Human_Safety_Protocol.title: 🛡️ Human Safety First
required_checkboxes: I am wearing my mask, long sleeves, and gloves.
The wind is calm, and I am standing with the wind at my back.
Children, pets, and food are removed from the spray area.
ppe_list: N95 Mask or Respirator (Protect your lungs)
Long-sleeved shirt and pants (Tucked into boots)

emergency_first_aid.on_skin: Drench skin with water for 15 mins. Remove clothes. Wash with soap.
emergency_first_aid.in_eyes: Hold eyelids open. Flush with clean water gently for 15 minutes.
emergency_first_aid.inhaled: Move to fresh air immediately. Loosen tight clothing.
emergency_first_aid.swallowed: Rinse mouth with water. Do NOT induce vomiting unless label says so.

diagnosis.description: Small, water-soaked greasy spots on leaves that turn brown/black with a yellow halo. Often caused by infected seeds or splashing water.
diagnosis.severity: High - Can cause total defoliation and fruit scabbing if untreated.

treatment.chemical: Copper-based fungicides (e.g., Kocide). Apply every 7-10 days in wet weather.
treatment.biological: Serenade ASO (Bacillus subtilis). Best used as a preventative measure.
treatment.steps: Spray in early morning before the sun is hot. Ensure full coverage of the underside of leaves.

cultural.sanitation: Remove and burn infected leaves immediately. Do not leave them on the soil.
cultural.environment: Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip lines. Increase plant spacing for better airflow.

prevention.rotation: Do not plant tomatoes, peppers, or potatoes in this spot for 2-3 years.
prevention.varieties: Use certified disease-free seeds. No 100% resistant variety is available yet.

diagnosis.description: Distinctive 'target' or 'bullseye' spots (concentric rings) on older leaves near the ground.
diagnosis.severity: Moderate to High - Spreads upwards and can reduce fruit yield significantly.

treatment.chemical: Fungicides containing Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb. Apply at first sign of symptoms.
treatment.biological: Neem oil sprays can help slow the spread in organic gardens.
treatment.steps: Prune the lower leaves that touch the soil to prevent soil-to-leaf transmission.

cultural.sanitation: Apply a layer of mulch (straw or plastic) to prevent soil spores from splashing onto leaves.
cultural.environment: Water at the base of the plant. Keep foliage as dry as possible.

prevention.rotation: Follow a 2-year rotation cycle away from Solanaceous crops.
prevention.varieties: Look for varieties labeled 'EB' (Early Blight) resistant like Mountain Magic.

diagnosis.description: Fast-moving disease with large, dark, water-soaked patches on leaves and stems. Can kill a plant in days.
diagnosis.severity: CRITICAL - Extremely contagious. Can destroy an entire field in a week.

treatment.chemical: Aggressive treatment with Ridomil Gold or fungicides containing Cyazofamid.
treatment.biological: Organic copper sprays are less effective once the disease is established.
treatment.steps: Check the local weather; high humidity and cool nights favor this disease. Act fast!

cultural.sanitation: IMMEDIATE ACTION: If infection is severe, bag the entire plant and remove it. Do not compost.
cultural.environment: Improve drainage and ensure the area is not overcrowded.

prevention.rotation: Eliminate 'volunteer' tomato plants and potato tubers that may carry spores over winter.
prevention.varieties: Choose late-blight resistant hybrids like 'Defiant' or 'Iron Lady'.

diagnosis.description: Olive-green to brown velvety mold on the underside of leaves with yellow spots on top.
diagnosis.severity: Moderate - Mostly affects greenhouse tomatoes with poor ventilation.

treatment.chemical: Fungicides like Difenoconazole. Often, chemical control is not needed if air is dry.
treatment.biological: Increase light exposure and reduce humidity below 85%.
treatment.steps: Focus on horizontal airflow. Use fans in greenhouses to keep air moving.

cultural.sanitation: Prune excess suckers to improve light penetration through the canopy.
cultural.environment: Lower the humidity! This fungus cannot grow in dry conditions.

prevention.rotation: Clean greenhouse structures thoroughly between seasons with a 10% bleach solution.
prevention.varieties: Select varieties with high resistance to Fulvia fulva (Leaf Mold).

diagnosis.description: Vibrant green leaves, no spotting or wilting. The plant is performing optimal photosynthesis.

treatment.chemical: None needed. Avoid unnecessary chemical use to protect beneficial insects.
treatment.biological: Apply compost tea or beneficial microbes to strengthen the root system.
treatment.steps: Continue monitoring once a week for early signs of pests or disease.

cultural.sanitation: Keep tools clean between handling different plants to avoid cross-contamination.
cultural.environment: Maintain consistent moisture to prevent Blossom End Rot.

prevention.rotation: Plan next year's crop layout now to ensure proper rotation.
prevention.varieties: Record which varieties performed best this year for future success.

Septoria_Leaf_Spot.diagnosis: Circular, greyish-white spots with dark borders. Starts on lower leaves.

treatment.chemical: Chlorothalonil or Copper-based fungicides. Apply every 7-14 days.
treatment.biological: Baking soda spray (1 tbsp per gallon) or potassium bicarbonate.
treatment.steps: Remove infected leaves immediately. Water at the base only to stop soil splash.

Septoria_Leaf_Spot.prevention: Stake plants to increase airflow and use mulch to keep soil away from leaves.

Spider_Mites.diagnosis: Tiny yellow stippling (dots) on leaves and fine webbing on the undersides.

treatment.biological: Neem oil or Insecticidal soaps. Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus).
treatment.steps: Blast the undersides of leaves with a strong water jet to dislodge mites.

Spider_Mites.prevention: Keep plants well-hydrated; mites love dry, dusty conditions.

Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.diagnosis: Leaves curl upwards, turn yellow at the edges, and look stunted/bushy.

treatment.chemical: No chemical cure for the virus. Use Imidacloprid to kill the Whiteflies that carry it.
treatment.biological: Yellow sticky traps to catch Whiteflies. Neem oil sprays.
treatment.steps: ROGUING: Immediately remove and burn infected plants to prevent the spread.

Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Use reflective silver mulch to repel Whiteflies. Plant resistant varieties.

Powdery_Mildew.diagnosis: White, flour-like powder on leaves and stems. Leaves may turn yellow and die.

treatment.biological: Milk spray (1 part milk to 9 parts water) or dilute apple cider vinegar.
treatment.steps: Apply sprays in the early morning. Ensure full coverage of both leaf sides.

Powdery_Mildew.prevention: Increase spacing between plants for better air movement. Avoid high humidity.

treatment.chemical: No cure. Focus on disinfecting tools with 10% bleach solution.
treatment.biological: None. Control aphids that can transmit the virus.
treatment.steps: Do not smoke near plants (tobacco carries mosaic). Remove infected plants immediately.

Mosaic_Virus.prevention: Wash hands with soap before touching plants. Use certified virus-free seeds.

Leaf_Miner.diagnosis: Slender, white, winding 'serpentine' tunnels inside the leaf tissue.

treatment.chemical: Spinosad or Abamectin. These penetrate the leaf to reach the larvae.
treatment.biological: Neem oil or Parasitic wasps (Diglyphus isaea).
treatment.steps: Squish the larvae inside the tunnels with your fingers if only a few leaves are affected.

Leaf_Miner.prevention: Use row covers to stop adult flies from laying eggs on leaves. Clean up crop debris.
